Study location: SeaSim aquarium facilities of the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS, Townsville, Queensland)
Sediment composition: primarily calcium carbonate from Davies Reef, 55 micron (PSD D50 value in Table 5)
Sediment notes: based on text in results on page 296
Binary Notes: based on minimal text in Results regarding bleaching presence/absence, partial mortality; not enough detail to know statistical significance of photosynthetic yield changes during recovery
Additional notes on study: complex study that mostly focuses on sediment- and flow-related drivers of sediment accumulation/removal; I focus on experiment S1 only, which measures photosynthetic yield as a function of sediment burial time and recovery time - note that response is given as % of control; if interested in recovery/removal, should return to this study to extract more data from experiment "C1-C3"
Rationale for inclusion: interesting and complex experimental set-up, with primary focus on sediment removal rates with different sediment types and flow rates -- fourth experiment (S1) and Fig. 7 show max quantum yield (as % of control) of 3 spp under fine and coarse silt; otherwise very limited binary information about bleaching; should return to extract data from this study if interested in sediment removal/accumulation

Study location: SeaSim aquarium facilities of the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS, Townsville, Queensland)
Sediment composition: primarily calcium carbonate from Davies Reef, 17 micron (PSD D50 value in Table 5)
Sediment notes: based on text in results on page 296
Binary Notes: based on minimal text in Results regarding bleaching presence/absence, partial mortality; limited detail concerning statistical significance of photosynthetic yield changes during recovery, but any yield within 10% of control (=100%) considered no effect (=0) and outside of 10% difference is considered an effect (=1)
Additional notes on study: complex study that mostly focuses on sediment- and flow-related drivers of sediment accumulation/removal; I focus on experiment S1 only, which measures photosynthetic yield as a function of sediment burial time and recovery time - note that response is given as % of control; if interested in recovery/removal, should return to this study to extract more data from experiment "C1-C3"
Rationale for inclusion: interesting and complex experimental set-up, with primary focus on sediment removal rates with different sediment types and flow rates -- fourth experiment (S1) and Fig. 7 show max quantum yield (as % of control) of 3 spp under fine and coarse silt; otherwise very limited binary information about bleaching; should return to extract data from this study if interested in sediment removal/accumulation

best_component_scores: {"Current genus name": 1.0, "Current species name": 1.0, "Sediment composition": 0.28, "Sediment level": 0.0}
Study location: Ma'alaea Bay, Maui, Hawaii, USA
Sediment composition: Carbonate sand and gravel (Table 2 for breakdown)
Sediment notes: Based on Fig. 3, which gives approximately monthly sediment levels (different from experimental increments, which were 2-4 month increments, see last sentence), so I averaged Sept-Oct for first period, Dec-FebB for second period, Mar-Apr for third period, and June-July for fourth period. Because I assume the mean is an average among 3 sediment traps at each site, I simply took an average of the average (and also averaged s.d.). Number of days exposed is based on descriptions of dates: "Aug-Oct" = 26 Aug to 18 Oct, "Oct-Feb" = 18 Oct to 22 Feb (not specified if Feb A or B), "Feb-Apr" = 22 Feb to 18 Apr, and "Apr-Jul" = 18 Apr to 18 July.
Binary Notes: reduced growth: based on neg or pos %change because no control comparison; necroses: based on mortality percentages - small necroses <50% tissue mortality, large necroses >=50% tissue mortality or any colony death (>0%), death of colonies is any colony death (>0%)
Additional notes on study: more detailed analysis of sediment than most/all other studies, and of coral responses; might be some difficulty in interpreting results because of lack of control due to this being a field experiment with many ambient conditions, which are also different at different sites --> might want to get original dataset so we can tease apart PAR, temp, and sediment
Rationale for inclusion: field experiment that exposes coral to range of natural conditions, particularly sediment exposure, and measures growth rate and tissue mortality; they also measure temperature and PAR --> might want to get original dataset so we can tease apart PAR, temp, and sediment; QUOTE: "The intent of this study was to measure coral growth along a range of sediment influence to test the hypothesis that coral growth would decrease with increasing sediment load, which this study did not observe. Instead there was a sig- nificant positive relationship between growth and sediment trap data, but the strength of the correlation (R2 1⁄4 0:387) suggests that fac- tors other than sediment accumulation were the primary controls of coral growth. The relationship between coral growth and sedi- ment may depend on how growth is mea- sured, how sediment load is quantified, and the intervals at which both are measured."

Exposure Duration or Timepoint: Light: 1380 microM/m2/s (max irradiance in winter at 10m depth, 50% light attenuation)
Study location: Maputaland coast, northern Natal, South Africa
Sediment composition: clean, mostly fine to medium sand (<500 micron) from nearby reefs
Sediment notes: Methods: "Of the clean sand, a constant quantity of 14.18 g, which amounted to a sediment load of 200 mg/cm-2 for the respiration chamber (bottom area= 70.9 cm2,therefore:70.9x 200 mg = 14.18g), was applied to the corals"
Binary Notes: reduction in P/R based on Table 2; mortality not mentioned
Additional notes on study: study focuses a great deal on light levels/reductions in irradiance, but I focus here on with/without sediment at one light level (1380 microM/m2/s) which is the max irradiance in winter at 10m depth (50% light attenuation); study also looked at soft corals, which I do not report here
Rationale for inclusion: laboratory study that focuses a great deal on physiological effects of light levels/reductions in irradiance crossed with sediment presence/absence; I report here sediment presence/absence at one light level (1380 microM/m2/s) which is the max irradiance in winter at 10m depth (50% light attenuation); study also looked at soft corals, which I do not report here

Study location: San Cristobal reef, Puerto Rico
Sediment composition: calcareous sediment from adjacent area with scarce living coral and consisting mostly of Halimeda plates; dried and weighed, then reapplied in the field as evenly as possible
Sediment notes: frequency of sediment application different, so rate is calculated to be average sediment load per day
Binary Notes: report small bleached areas, but these also appeared on controls!, so I have put zeros, to represent lack of difference among treatments and control
Additional notes on study: this study also conducted a dose experiment, but did not quantitatively report the results -- check Rogers dissertation for more details
Rationale for inclusion: field experiment that looked at growth rates of coral exposed to different dose-frequencies of sediment, but found no effect of sediment; relatively small sample size, but individual level data available in Table IV; this study perhaps more useful for its experiment looking at light limitation's effects on growth, metabolism

Binary Notes: P/R and Bleaching based on Fig. 1 which shows no difference between controls and sediment-treated corals (main effect is between symbiotic/asymbiotic); Fig. 2 for growth rate (same pattern as P/R); Results text for necroses and mucus
Additional notes on study: no responses in first four weeks of study; the study continued and found declines in coral health after starvation in addition to sediment; otherwise, this coral was a good sediment shedder
Rationale for inclusion: lab experiment, but worked with temperate scleractinian coral species instead of tropical one
